item.title: US, Indonesia discussing agreement allowing US military overflight in Indonesian airspace, Indonesian ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Indonesia and ‚ÄĆthe United States are discussing ‚Äčan ‚Äčagreement to give U.S. ‚Ā†military aircraft ‚Äčaccess to ‚ÄčIndonesian airspace, Indonesia's defence ministry said ‚Äčon Monday, ‚Äčadding that no deal ‚ÄĆhas ‚Ā†yet been reached.
U.S. Defence Secretary Pete ‚ÄčHegseth ‚Äčis ‚Ā†set to meet ‚Äčwith his ‚ÄčIndonesian ‚Ā†counterpart Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin later ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčMonday.

item.title: Two killed in Ukraine despite Easter ceasefire, governor says
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Two people were killed and one injured by Russian troops in Ukraine's frontline eastern Donetsk region ‚Äčbefore the expiry of a ceasefire to mark Orthodox ‚ÄĆEaster , a local official said on Monday,
One person was killed in the city of Kramatorsk and one in Druzhkivka ‚Äčon Sunday, regional governor Vadym Filashkin said on ‚Äčthe Telegram messaging app, without giving more details.
Both ‚Ā†Russia and Ukraine have accused each other of breaching ‚Äčthe 32-hour ceasefire from Saturday afternoon to midnight (2100 GMT) ‚Äčon Sunday.
Ukraine's Coordination Headquarters for the Treatment of Prisoners of War, citing a spokesperson of the 14th Army Corps, said on ‚ÄčMonday that during "the so-called 'Easter ceasefire'" Russian servicemen also ‚Äčshot dead four Ukrainian prisoners of war in the Kharkiv region.
It ‚ÄĆdid ‚Ā†not give more details, adding that Russia was preparing a campaign to deny it.
There was no immediate comment from Russia and Reuters could not verify the ‚Äčinformation independently.
The General ‚ÄčStaff of ‚Ā†Ukraine's military said on Monday it had compiled a total of 10,721 Russian ‚Äčviolations over the course of the truce.
It ‚Äčsaid the ‚Ā†violations included 1,567 artillery shelling incidents, 119 assault operations and 9,035 strikes by attack drones. It said no ‚Ā†air ‚Äčstrikes had been recorded.
The Russian ‚Äčdefence ministry has said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations by Ukraine overnight ‚Äčinto Sunday.

item.title: Kuwait raises May crude prices for Asia, pricing document shows
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Kuwait has raised ‚Äčthe official selling ‚ÄĆprice for Kuwait Export ‚ÄčCrude to ‚ÄčAsia in May ‚Ā†to $17 a ‚Äčbarrel above ‚Äčthe average of Oman/Dubai quotes, a ‚Äčprice document ‚Äčreviewed by Reuters ‚ÄĆshowed ‚Ā†on Monday.
The producer increased the May Kuwait ‚ÄčSuper ‚ÄčLight ‚Ā†crude OSP to $17 ‚Äča barrel ‚Äčabove ‚Ā†Oman/Dubai quotes.

item.title: Poland‚Äôs premier hails Hungary vote as blow to authoritarian rule
item.description:
WARSAW, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's election shows that Europe is not on an inevitable path toward authoritarian rule, ‚ÄčPolish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Monday, ‚ÄĆafter the center-right Tisza party ended Viktor Orban's 16‚ÄĎyear grip on power.
"Everyone feared there was a trend toward authoritarian, corrupt ‚Äčregimes," Tusk said while on an official ‚Äčvisit to South Korea , according to Polish state-owned ‚Ā†news agency PAP.
"That's not the case. First Warsaw, ‚Äčthen Bucharest, Chisinau, now Budapest."
In 2025, centrist Nicusor Dan won ‚Äča presidential election in Romania and Moldova's pro-European ruling party won a resounding victory over its Russia-aligned rival.
Tusk had repeatedly criticized ‚ÄčOrban's government for its close ties with Moscow.
"I'm ‚Äčglad that this part of Europe is showing that we ‚ÄĆare ‚Ā†not doomed to corrupt and authoritarian governments, because that's what Victor Orban's government unfortunately became after many years in power."
Neighbors Poland and Hungary are linked ‚Äčby their long, ‚Äčshared history, ‚Ā†close trade ties and cooperation within the European Union and NATO.
Tusk said he ‚Äčhad spoken with Tisza leader Peter ‚ÄčMagyar ‚Ā†to congratulate him on his victory.
"We briefly discussed his visit to Warsaw," Tusk said.
"As you know, he long ‚Ā†ago ‚Äčchose Warsaw as his first ‚Äčvisit for quite obvious reasons. I think our relationship will be ‚Äčabsolutely exceptional."

item.title: Philippines warns of 'sabotage' after cyanide seizure in disputed South China Sea atoll
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines discovered cyanide on Chinese boats operating around a ‚ÄĆdisputed atoll in the South China Sea, security officials from the country said on Monday.
Authorities said laboratory tests confirmed the presence of the highly toxic substance in bottles seized by the Philippine navy in operations at Second ‚ÄčThomas Shoal last year.
Officials warned the cyanide could have had serious consequences for marine life ‚Äčand weakened the reef supporting a warship that Manila grounded on the ‚Ā†atoll to reinforce its maritime claim.
"We wish to underscore that the use of cyanide in ‚ÄčAyungin Shoal is a form of sabotage that seeks to kill local fish populations, depriving ‚ÄčNavy personnel of a vital food source," Cornelio Valencia, National Security Council spokesperson, told a press conference using the Philippines' name for the atoll. Valencia added that cyanide could damage the reef and "ultimately compromise" the warship's stability.
The ‚ÄčChinese Embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Philippines has ‚Äčaccused China of disrupting resupply missions to troops on the vessel, including a June 17, 2024, incident that ‚ÄĆturned ‚Ā†violent and resulted in a Filipino sailor losing a finger. China has denied allegations of aggressive conduct during such encounters and accused the Philippines of trespassing in its waters.
The June confrontation later led to a provisional understanding for resupply missions to the grounded ship.
China and the ‚ÄčPhilippines held high-level talks ‚Äčlast month over the ‚Ā†South China Sea, exploring preliminary steps towards oil and gas cooperation , and confidence-building measures at sea, including communication between their coast guards.
The Philippine foreign ministry ‚Äčsaid in a statement on Monday the scope of the coast ‚Äčguard cooperation ‚Ā†would be limited, and "does not contemplate cooperation in sensitive operational areas," adding that there had been no discussions on joint patrols.
China claims nearly all the South China Sea, including areas claimed by ‚Ā†Brunei, Malaysia, ‚Äčthe Philippines and Vietnam.
A 2016 ruling by an international ‚Äčarbitral tribunal found Beijing's sweeping claims had no basis under international law, a decision China rejects. More than $3 trillion ‚Äčin annual ship-borne commerce travels through the key waterway.

item.title: Exclusive: Chilean copper miner Codelco, contractors fined after deadly mine collapse
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Chilean state copper miner Codelco was fined by labor authorities after last year's deadly collapse at its El Teniente mine, while three contractors whose workers were killed or injured were sanctioned more heavily, according to inspection records obtained by Reuters through public-records requests.
The previously unreported sanctions were imposed in the months ‚Äčfollowing the July 31 underground seismic event that triggered a rock burst at El Teniente , the world's largest underground copper mine, killing six contract workers and injuring others.
Reuters obtained the files from ‚ÄĆChile's labor ministry through open-records requests. In Chile, such fines are notified directly to employers and can be challenged or reduced administratively, but are not typically disclosed in public announcements.
At the time of the accident, then-Labor Minister Giorgio Boccardo said that his office and the mining regulator, Sernageomin, would investigate the causes of the incident and whether any labor-safety rules had been breached.
The quake, measured at about magnitude 4.3, led to a halt in all underground operations at the sprawling mine complex amid rescue efforts and safety inspections.
The collapse ‚Äčcarried a significant production cost for Codelco. The company has said the shutdown of underground operations at El Teniente and a slow restart cut copper output by tens of thousands of metric tons, disrupting shipments at ‚Äča time of tight global supply.
The disaster also underscored the geotechnical risks facing Chile's aging underground mines .
The records show the three contractors were fined the ‚Ā†equivalent of about $87,000 combined, versus roughly $20,000 for Codelco, a gap that reflects Chile's split-liability framework for subcontracted work.
While the principal company - Codelco - can be penalized for overarching safety failures, contractors remain directly responsible as employers for accident reporting, ‚Äčrisk assessment, worker assignment and other compliance duties, under Chile's labor code.
In one sanction, labor inspectors said Codelco lacked a complete, written procedure showing how seismic warnings were used to decide whether work should stop or be restricted.
After the ‚Äčaccident, regulators also found that Codelco violated labor rules when workers were found entering, or preparing to enter, underground areas while the mine-wide suspension remained in force, according to a separate sanction record.
Under Chilean labor rules, some serious or fatal-accident violations can be fined at up to 150 UTM, an inflation-linked Chilean tax unit, or about $11,000 at current values, per infraction. The regulator levied a 340 UTM penalty, roughly $26,000 in today's terms, on a company after a fatal construction-site collapse in a 2007 case.
Labor advocates and worker-safety specialists ‚Äčhave questioned whether such small penalties carry enough deterrent force for major employers.
"It is essential to raise the size of fines in order to effectively deter companies from violating mining safety regulations," a Chilean House of Representatives investigative ‚Äčcommission report said in 2011 after a mining disaster.
Proposals since then to hike up fines for serious or fatal workplace accidents have not gotten off the ground.
Since the collapse, Codelco has said it tightened safety procedures for restarting work at ‚ÄĆEl Teniente, ‚Ā†adding safety briefings at the start of shifts, improving communications underground, stepping up checks on workers' locations and reviewing protective equipment.
It later said an independent panel led by a former Anglo American CEO was examining what caused the accident and whether broader management or workplace problems played a role.
In a statement to Reuters, Codelco said that its seismic alert response system was active the day of the accident, and that it had appealed the fine by the labor ministry.
It added that there was an "ongoing legal proceeding related to the oversight of worker entry during the work stoppage" and that it was awaiting a decision from authorities.
Codelco said in August that El Teniente mine manager Andres Music would leave ‚Äčhis role, and in February announced the departure of three ‚Äčsenior executives after an internal audit found inconsistencies and ‚Ā†concealment following a separate rock burst at the mine several years earlier.
Among the three contracting firms fined, Strabag (STRV.VI) , opens new tab subsidiary Zublin was fined for failing to report a worker's death within 24 hours. Inspectors found that although the company knew about the fatality within two hours, it didn't notify labor authorities until the following evening.
Immediate reporting ‚Äčis critical to implement emergency safety measures to protect remaining workers, the report said.
The Austrian company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A unit of ‚ÄčChilean construction firm SalfaCorp (SALFACORP.SN) , opens new tab was also ‚Ā†sanctioned after one of its workers died in the Andesita sector of the mine. Inspectors said the company failed to immediately report the fatal accident to authorities, in addition to other offenses.
The records also said Salfa's risk assessment did not adequately account for seismic hazards and that the company failed to do enough to protect workers' lives.
SalfaCorp did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Chile's labor regulator also fined Constructora Gardilcic, the unlisted contractor whose workers were killed and injured in ‚Ā†the Recursos Norte ‚Äčarea of the mine.
Inspectors said the company reported the accident late, delayed filing injury reports and showed weak safety planning.
Authorities also concluded Gardilcic ‚Äčdid not adequately account for the risk of violent rock bursts outside designated danger zones and put some workers in jobs they were not cleared to do.
Gardilcic did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Codelco has said the areas hardest hit by the accident ‚Äčwill remain under tight restrictions while criminal , regulatory and technical investigations continue.
The company has pledged a gradual, regulator-approved restart, leaving uncertainty over when the entire mine can resume normal operations.

item.title: UK will not back blockade of Strait of Hormuz, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on ‚ÄčMonday that whatever the pressure, Britain would not be dragged into ‚ÄĆthe Iran war nor be involved in a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
"We're not supporting the blockade," he told BBC Radio 5 Live, adding that it was ‚Äčvital to get the Strait reopened.
"It is, in my view, vital ‚Äčthat we get the Strait open and fully open, and ‚Ā†that's where we've put all of our efforts in the last ‚Äčfew and we'll continue to do so," Starmer said.
Britain had minesweepers in the ‚Äčregion, he said, and while he could not discuss operational matters, the military capability was "focused from our point of view on getting the Strait full open".
The U.S. military said it ‚Äčwould begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting ‚ÄčIranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after weekend talks failed to reach a deal ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†end the war with Iran.
The U.S. Central Command said that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET on Monday (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ‚Äčareas, including all ‚ÄčIranian ports on ‚Ā†the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ‚Äčnot be impeded, the U.S. military said.
President Donald Trump ‚Äčsaid on ‚Ā†Sunday U.S. forces would also intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will ‚Ā†have safe ‚Äčpassage on the high seas," Trump wrote ‚Äčon social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be ‚ÄčBLOWN TO HELL!"

item.title: Iran's Hormuz gamble ushers in a tense new normal for Gulf energy
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have shattered a status quo that prevailed among Middle East oil and gas producers for decades. Even if the current, shaky ceasefire holds, the uneasy ‚Äúnew normal‚ÄĚ has likely set the stage for yet another round of conflict.
Tehran has now demonstrated both its ability and willingness to seal off the critical waterway and strike vital ‚Äčinfrastructure across the region, fundamentally altering the risk calculus for its neighbours and jeopardizing Gulf countries‚Äô long‚ÄĎterm oil and gas strategies.
The six‚ÄĎweek war laid bare deep tensions between the Islamic Republic and its ‚ÄĆkey regional neighbours Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain and Iraq - all close U.S. allies. They had long avoided direct confrontation with Tehran under a tacit understanding that war would devastate their shared economic interests. But that entente cordiale has now been blown apart.
At the heart of the crisis lies the Strait of Hormuz . Iran closed the waterway for the first time in history, trapping nearly a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas supplies inside the Gulf, delivering a devastating shock to the region and the global economy. Even if shipping ultimately resumes, ‚Äčthe unprecedented act marks a historic rupture.
Iran has signalled that it wants to retain leverage over the strait under any future peace deal, floating the idea of controlling traffic and charging vessels a transit fee. U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump has urged Tehran to fully reopen the waterway, and on Sunday said the U.S. Navy would immediately start blockading the strait, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to ‚Ā†reach a deal to end the war .
Yet even a formal reopening would do little to erase the lesson the war has taught Iran‚Äôs neighbours. The threat of Iran closing the strait - now proven feasible with limited military effort - is a genie ‚Äčthat cannot be put back into the bottle.
The war has sharply exposed how vulnerable the region‚Äôs energy infrastructure is.
Fighting between the U.S., Israel and Iran quickly spilled across borders as Iranian missiles and drones struck dozens of targets in neighbouring ‚Äčcountries, including major oil and gas facilities across the Gulf.
Around 11 million barrels per day of oil production and Qatar‚Äôs entire LNG output were forced offline, while refineries, export terminals and other critical installations , opens new tab suffered extensive damage.
For Saudi Arabia, the world‚Äôs largest crude exporter and de facto leader of OPEC, the implications are especially troubling. Even the alternative export route it painstakingly developed in recent decades to bypass Hormuz proved vulnerable.
The kingdom‚Äôs East‚ÄĎWest Pipeline, designed to divert roughly 7 million barrels per day from the eastern oil heartland to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ‚Äčwas hit within hours of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement.
Saudi Arabia, which exported around 8 million bpd of oil before the war, said the attacks had cut its oil production capacity by around 600,000 bpd and reduced throughput on the East‚ÄĎWest Pipeline by about ‚Äč700,000 bpd.
At the same time, the UAE‚Äôs oil export pipeline to the port of Fujairah , which sits outside the strait, was also struck repeatedly. And for Qatar and Kuwait, the strait remains the sole export route.
While this situation might look like a checkmate for Tehran, ‚ÄĆthe Gulf is ‚Ā†more apt to see it as an intolerable status quo that will need to be remedied.
This new reality strikes at the core of the Gulf‚Äôs economic model ‚Äď both now and in the future.
For starters, the region faces years of reconstruction and instability that will undermine national development plans, even if the elevated geopolitical risk premium keeps energy prices higher.
The long-term threat is even greater.
The war has jolted many nations - particularly in Asia - into a painful reassessment of energy import dependence. For the Gulf, that comes at the worst possible moment: producers were already facing the need to maximise exports in the face of potentially diminishing demand as major importers shift away from fossil fuels. The conflict will only accelerate it.
For Gulf producers who ‚Äčexpect uninterrupted exports of oil, liquefied natural gas, refined ‚Äčproducts, chemicals and fertilizer to fund their economies for ‚Ā†decades to come, uncertainty over Hormuz is simply unsustainable, not only geopolitically but economically.
Consequently, the key regional powers, Saudi Arabia and the UAE - both of which harbour international ambitions economically and geopolitically - are unlikely to accept any strategic reality in which Iran can constrain their goals over the long term. That, in turn, points to a heightened risk of future confrontation.
"The strait ‚Äčmust be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand," ‚ÄčSultan Al Jaber, the CEO of ‚Ā†UAE state oil giant ADNOC, said last week .
The terms of whatever "permanent" peace deal the parties eventually agree to will matter less than the fact that this war has broken a long‚ÄĎstanding taboo: targeting vital economic infrastructure in the Gulf is no longer off limits.
That shift alone makes the regional balance of power more volatile, casting a long shadow over the Middle East for years to come.
The rally in global equities and the decline in oil prices following last week's ceasefire announcement suggest investors believe the Middle ‚Ā†East will soon ‚Äčreturn to business as usual.
They are almost certainly wrong. The most likely outcome is not a restoration of the old order but a new ‚Äčnormal - one in which Gulf powers seek to ensure Iran can never again wield the Hormuz trump card.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ‚Äčdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Iran, US 'sincere' about ceasefire: Turkey foreign minister
item.description:
PAKISTAN-WAR-IRAN-US-ISRAEL

Turkey's foreign minister said on Monday he believed both Iran and the United States remain "sincere" about reaching a ceasefire despite the failure of Pakistan-mediated talks on the weekend.

US President Donald Trump has blamed the Islamic republic's refusal to abandon its nuclear ambitions for the collapse of the talks.

"Both sides are sincere about the ceasefire," Hakan Fidan said in an interview with the official Anadolu news agency, adding that he has been in contact with the parties involved in the negotiations.

"I am thinking that if the nuclear issue comes down to an all-or-nothing situation especially regarding enrichment, we might face serious obstacles," Fidan said.

"Hopefully, we will try to overcome this with the support of some mediators," he added.

Turkey, while a fierce critic of Israel, joined diplomatic efforts with Egypt and Pakistan to reach a ceasefire in the conflict.

item.title: Israeli fire kills three in Gaza following new ceasefire talks
item.description:
CAIRO/GAZA, April 13 (Reuters) - An Israeli airstrike killed at ‚Äčleast three Palestinians in the Gaza Strip on Monday, health officials said, as mediators met leaders from ‚ÄĆHamas in an effort to shore up a U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal.
Medics said the strike had hit a group of men outside a school in Deir al-Balah in the central Gaza Strip. There was no immediate comment by the Israeli military.
At Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir al-Balah, the bodies ‚Äčof those killed lay on the ground in white shrouds outside the morgue as relatives and friends arrived ‚Äčto bid them farewell. Some kissed the victims' foreheads before holding special prayers.
‚ÄúThis isn‚Äôt a truce; ‚Ā†it‚Äôs a trap for our young men. Every day there are martyrs, every single day. How long can this continue?‚ÄĚ ‚Äčsaid Umm Hussam Abu El-Rous, a female relative of one of the victims.
‚ÄúIsn't it unjust that a three-year-old child is afraid ‚Äčof seeing his (dead) father? He says, 'My father went to bring me something from the shop,'" she added.
The ceasefire that began last October halted two years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone demarcated by yellow-painted blocks that comprises well over half of ‚ÄčGaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip and Israeli airstrikes continuing .
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since ‚Äčthe deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli soldiers. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for ceasefire violations.
Palestinians say Israeli forces ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†been moving some of the yellow concrete markers westward. Israel denies this.
The violence comes as leaders from Hamas and other Palestinian factions have been meeting since Saturday in Cairo with mediators from Egypt, Turkey, and Qatar to discuss implementing the second phase of the Gaza deal.
Under a plan put forward by U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace , Hamas would be ‚Äčrequired to lay down its arms ‚Äčin stages over eight months ‚Ā†after a U.S.-backed committee of Palestinian technocrats takes control in Gaza.
However, Hamas' disarmament has been a major obstacle to progress on Trump's ceasefire deal and plan for Gaza , which have also ‚Äčbeen put under strain by the war in Iran.
Two officials close to the latest talks ‚Äčsaid Hamas told ‚Ā†mediators that discussions on disarmament could only move forward after Israel fully implements the first phase of Trump's October deal, which includes a complete ceasefire in Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does ‚Ā†not ‚Äčlay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began on October 7, 2023, with a ‚ÄčHamas-led attack on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, according to Gazan health authorities, ‚Äčand left the territory mostly in ruins.

item.title: ASEAN foreign ministers urge US and Iran to push for permanent resolution
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign ministers called on Monday for the United States ‚Äčand Iran to continue negotiating a permanent ‚ÄĆend to their conflict, and to implement the ceasefire fully and effectively.
The ministers, who met virtually to discuss the war ‚Äčin the Middle East, also called for the restoration of ‚Äčsafe, unimpeded and continuous passage of vessels and ‚Ā†aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Kremlin says it hopes for pragmatic ties with new Hungarian leadership after Orban defeat
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Monday it hoped to continue its "highly pragmatic ties" with ‚ÄčHungary's new political leadership after Prime Minister ‚ÄĆViktor Orban, a close Russian partner, was beaten at the ballot box by upstart rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, Hungary's veteran nationalist leader, lost power to Magyar ‚Äčand his centre-right Tisza party in an ‚Äčelection on Sunday after 16 years in office - ‚Ā†a setback for his allies in Russia and ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
"Hungary has made its ‚Äčchoice, and we respect that choice," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"We look forward to continuing our highly pragmatic engagement ‚Äčwith Hungary‚Äôs new leadership. We noted Magyar‚Äôs statement ‚Äčregarding his willingness to engage in dialogue. Naturally, this will be ‚ÄĆbeneficial ‚Ā†for both Moscow and Budapest."
Orban, who last visited Moscow in November, blocked a 90-billion euro ($105 billion) loan to Ukraine after accusing it of sabotaging Russian oil ‚Äčdeliveries to ‚Äčhis country, ‚Ā†something Kyiv denied, and delayed and sometimes opposed EU sanctions on Russia.
Hungary has ‚Äčalso continued to buy oil and gas ‚Äčfrom ‚Ā†Russia despite Moscow's war in Ukraine - which Orban said he did not want his country to be dragged ‚Ā†into - ‚Äčand Moscow is building a new ‚Äčnuclear power plant for Hungary.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Ghana says France is open to engaging on slavery reparations
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Ghana said France was open to having discussions with a coalition of countries that are calling for reparations for transatlantic slavery, following a meeting ‚Äčlast week with President Emmanuel Macron.
Ghana's President John Dramani Mahama, accompanied ‚ÄĆby foreign minister Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa and other officials, held talks with Macron in Paris on Wednesday last week.
Ablakwa said on X after the meeting that Macron has indicated ‚ÄčFrance was open to discussions on reparations, including the return of ‚Äčlooted artefacts, addressing global economic inequities and dismantling structural racism.
An official ‚Ā†from √Člys√©e Palace said on Sunday that both countries discussed France's efforts ‚Äčto return culturally significant objects and human remains, as well as the legal ‚Äčframeworks around these restitutions.
The official did not mention the additional measures cited by Ablakwa.
The meeting followed the United Nations' adoption last month of a Ghana-led resolution recognising slavery as the "gravest crime ‚Äčagainst humanity" and calling for reparations. France, along with other European countries, abstained.
France's ‚Äčrepresentative at the U.N. said the abstention was due to concerns that the resolution appeared ‚ÄĆto "establish ‚Ā†a hierarchy among crimes against humanity".
Ablakwa said that despite that abstention, Macron had said France was willing to have an "open and honest dialogue" on the matter.
In 2001, France recognised transatlantic slavery as a crime against humanity. But, like most ‚ÄčEuropean nations, it ‚Äčhas not formally ‚Ā†apologised for its involvement or committed to reparations.
From the 15th to the 19th century, at least 12.5 million Africans were ‚Äčkidnapped and forcibly transported by mostly European ships and ‚Äčsold into ‚Ā†slavery. France trafficked an estimated 1.3 million people, according to the Slave Voyages database.
Last year, Macron said he would set up a commission to examine France's past with ‚Ā†Haiti.
Longstanding ‚Äčcalls for reparations have gained momentum worldwide but so ‚Äčhas a backlash , with critics arguing modern states should not be held accountable for historical wrongs.

item.title: Germany plans $1.9 billion of fuel price relief to tackle energy crisis
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters) - Germany's coalition government has agreed fuel price relief for consumers and businesses worth ‚ÄĆ1.6 billion euros ($1.9 billion), ending a dispute over how to respond to an oil price surge triggered by the Iran war .
The energy tax on diesel and petrol will be cut by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, the conservative CDU party and its ‚Äčcentre-left SPD coalition partners said on Monday.
The Iran war has caused the biggest disruption to global energy supplies ‚Äčon record, with plans for a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas further driving ‚Ā†up crude prices .
"This war is the real cause of the problems we are experiencing in our own ‚Äčcountry as well," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said at a press conference.
He said the coalition was doing everything possible to ‚Äčcushion the impact of the conflict, which has been put on hold by a fragile ceasefire, and urged oil companies to pass the tax cut on in full. "We expect the oil industry to pass along these relief measures directly and fully to consumers," Merz ‚Äčsaid.
Economists and industry groups were sceptical.
Marcel Fratzscher at economic research institute DIW Berlin said a large share of the ‚Äčtax break could "end up in oil companies' bank accounts" and criticised the measures for failing to encourage fuel-saving.
Germany's filling station operators ‚ÄĆechoed that ‚Ā†concern, calling on the government to impose price controls on oil majors or risk them marking up prices to pocket part of the relief.
"The government needs to be tough on oil majors," a spokesperson told the Rheinische Post newspaper.
The coalition also agreed to let companies pay a 1,000 euro relief bonus per employee, exempt from payroll taxes ‚Äčand social security contributions.
Weekend talks ‚Äčappeared to defuse a dispute ‚Ā†that flared on Friday when Economy Minister Katherina Reiche, a Merz ally, criticised proposals from Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil of the SPD to impose a windfall tax on oil companies.
A ‚Äčsource close to Merz told Reuters that Reiche's remarks undermined a push by the ‚Äčchancellor to resolve ‚Ā†coalition disagreements quietly.
The government is under pressure to act as Europe's largest economy struggles with weak growth and disruptions from global trade tensions.
Merz said on Monday that Germany would oppose a planned 2027 tightening of European Union CO2 levies on ‚Ā†hybrid ‚Äčvehicles and would argue in Brussels for a more "technology-open" approach, including recognition ‚Äčof cars running on renewable fuels.
The coalition is also preparing broader income tax cuts for lower- and middle-income groups from January 2027.
($1 = 0.8556 ‚Äčeuros)

item.title: Japan risks summer power crunch due to Middle East LNG disruptions -IEEJ analyst
item.description:
TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Japan may face a power supply crunch if the Middle East crisis drags on and liquefied natural gas shipments remain disrupted, just as ‚Äčair-conditioning demand rises during the Japanese summer, an analyst at a leading ‚ÄĆenergy think tank said on Monday.
Takafumi Yanagisawa, executive analyst at the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan (IEEJ), said that a prolonged conflict could also delay the start of additional supplies expected by Japan ‚Äčfrom 2028 from new projects in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Nigeria seeks IMF, World Bank support as Iran shock hits reforms
item.description:
ABUJA, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria will seek stronger international financial support at this week's IMF-World Bank ‚ÄĆSpring Meetings as the Iran war lifts fuel costs at home and complicates reforms, its finance minister said on Monday.
Surging crude prices had some clear benefits for Africa's ‚Äčtop oil producer, boosting foreign exchange earnings, Wale Edun said in ‚Äča statement ahead of this week's meetings in Washington.
"But the ‚Ā†shock comes at a critical transition point, intensifying inflationary pressures and ‚Äčraising living costs for households," he added.
Petrol prices have surged more than ‚Äč50% to 1,330 naira ($0.9788) per litre - and diesel more than 70% to 1,550 naira a litre - since the start of the conflict, hurting people and businesses, Edun said.
The jolting ‚Äčchange threatened to derail reforms launched in 2023 to stabilise the economy ‚Äčand revive growth.
President Bola Tinubu has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in ‚ÄĆdecades ‚Ā†by ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system.
At this week's meetings, Edun, who chairs the G24 group of developing countries, said he planned to push for lower borrowing costs, ‚Äčfairer global financial conditions ‚Äčand more ‚Ā†support for countries pursuing reforms.
Nigeria's benchmark Bonny Light crude grade climbed from about $70‚Äď$73 a barrel before the conflict ‚Äčto current levels above $120, the government said.
Inflation eased sharply ‚Äčto 15.06% ‚Ā†in February from around 33% in December 2024, but remains high compared with regional peers and has come under renewed pressure since the conflict began, ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčWorld Bank said last week.
Edun said the ‚Äčgovernment would focus on attracting private investment, creating jobs and sustaining growth, while shielding vulnerable households ‚Äčfrom rising prices.
($1 = 1,358.8200 naira)

item.title: Philippines' Marcos suspends taxes on fuel products
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum ‚Äčgas on Monday to help consumers cope with ‚ÄĆrising fuel costs.
Congress had granted Marcos emergency powers to adjust fuel taxes, one of several measures the Philippines has ‚Äčundertaken to cope with a sharp surge ‚Äčin oil prices driven by the Middle East ‚Ā†conflict.
Marcos said the temporary tax reduction cuts LPG prices by 3.36 ‚ÄčPhilippine pesos ($0.0559) per kilogram and almost 37 Philippine pesos ($0.6154) per tank ‚Äčand 5.60 Philippine pesos ($0.0931) per liter, lowering costs for households that rely on it for lighting and cooking.
Marcos did not ‚Äčsay how long the suspension will last, but ‚Äčan interagency committee tasked with leading the government's response to ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†energy crisis will meet on April 14 to discuss whether to suspend, reduce, or maintain excise taxes on gasoline and diesel.
Diesel and gasoline prices in the ‚ÄčPhilippines have ‚Äčmore than ‚Ā†doubled since the start of the Middle East conflict on February 28, as ‚Äčthe country, which relies heavily on imported ‚Äčfuel, ‚Ā†faces supply pressures amid a global price surge.
The Philippines' central bank warned last week of "spillover effects" from the Middle ‚Ā†East ‚Äčconflict after headline inflation in ‚ÄčMarch hit 4.1% on an annual basis.
($1 = 60.1270 Philippine pesos)

item.title: EU should reconsider its plans to ban imports of Russian gas, Eni CEO says
item.description:
MILAN, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union should reconsider its plans to ‚Äčprogressively ban imports of Russian ‚ÄĆgas from the start of 2027, Claudio Descalzi, the CEO of Italian ‚Äčenergy group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said.
In ‚Äčan address to an event organised ‚Ā†by the co-ruling League ‚Äčparty on Sunday, Descalzi was quoted ‚Äčby Italian news agencies as saying it was unclear how the bloc could ‚Äčreplace 20 billion cubic ‚Äčmetres of gas from Russia, which provides ‚ÄĆvital ‚Ā†flexibility to the output of power stations.
Russia is facing a European ban on Russian LNG imports ‚Äčon short-term ‚Äčcontracts ‚Ā†from April 25 and on long-term contracts from ‚ÄčJanuary 1, 2027.
Descalzi said ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†in terms of the disruption of energy supplies, the conflict in ‚Ā†Iran ‚Äčis "the most important ‚Äčevent of the last 40 years."

item.title: India's HPCL issues rare tender seeking tanker to load Russian LPG, document shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's state-run Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab has issued a rare ‚Äčtender seeking a liquefied petroleum gas tanker ‚ÄĆto immediately load propane and butane from Russia's Ust-Luga port, a tender document seen by Reuters ‚Äčon Monday showed.
The vessel, to be loaded ‚Äčwith 12,000 metric tons of butane and ‚Ā†8,000 tons of propane for discharge ‚Äčon India's west coast, must not be under ‚Äčsanctions and must have no links to Iran, the document said.
HPCL did not immediately respond to a ‚Äčrequest for comment.
Indian state refiners are seeking ‚Äčto buy LPG, used as cooking gas, from more diversified ‚ÄĆsources ‚Ā†including Russia as India faces its worst LPG supply crisis in decades.
Supplies from the Middle East have been disrupted by the ‚Äčclosure of the ‚ÄčStrait of ‚Ā†Hormuz following the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Indian authorities have previously said they ‚Äčare purchasing LPG from countries including ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†U.S., Norway, Canada, and Russia.
India consumed 33.15 million tons of LPG last year, with imports ‚Ā†accounting ‚Äčfor about 60% of ‚Äčdemand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle ‚ÄčEast.

item.title: CPC raises oil exports in March by 48% from February, sources say
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), which supplies oil from Kazakhstan and Russia, increased crude exports by 48% in March, two industry ‚Äčsources said on Monday, buoyed by a recovery ‚Äčin output at the Tengiz oilfield.
Chevron-led (CVX.N) , opens new tab Tengiz is the ‚Ā†largest single field in Kazakhstan, a country that produces ‚Äč2% of the world's daily supply of crude oil, mainly ‚Äčexported via CPC to the Russian port of Novorossiysk.
The field, operated by Tengizchevroil, went offline in January after power outages were reported ‚Äčat the site near the Caspian Sea in western ‚ÄčKazakhstan.
Tengizchevroil's general director, William Lacobie, said last week that the field had returned ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†full production.
The sources said that CPC, which pumps oil via the Tengiz-to-Novorossiysk pipeline, increased oil exports last month to 6.196 million metric tons, or 1.58 million barrels per day. ‚ÄčThat compared with ‚ÄčFebruary's 3.79 ‚Ā†million tons, or 1.07 million bpd.
CPC declined to comment.
Challenges facing CPC have included Ukrainian ‚Äčdrone attacks on the Russian terminal. It had ‚Äčto ‚Ā†halt its operations for a few days after Ukraine attacked its facilities at the end of November, effectively destroying one of ‚Ā†three ‚Äčfloating moorings.
Kazakhstan's energy ministry said on ‚ÄčTuesday that oil exports via CPC were stable after Russia's military had accused Ukraine ‚Äčof damaging its loading facilities.

item.title: India's top solar state has renewable projects of about 60 GW awaiting transmission links
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's top solar energy-generating state of Rajasthan has clean energy projects of capacity amounting to ‚Äčabout 60 gigawatt (GW) awaiting transmission links as planners struggle ‚ÄĆto keep pace with a rapid build-out, a regulatory filing showed.
The problem underscores a critical challenge for India in its effort to nearly double its ‚Äčnon-fossil based power generation to 500 GW by 2030 ‚Äčas the systems carry electricity to other states from ‚Ā†renewable‚ÄĎrich regions such as Rajasthan.
Planner the Central Transmission Utility of India ‚ÄčLtd (CTUIL), told the national power regulator it had been unable to ‚Äčprovide a transmission system for about 60 GW of projects.
The western desert state has 179 GW of renewable energy potential, with more than 85% of ‚Äčprojects clustered in its four districts of Barmer, Bikaner, Jaisalmer ‚Äčand Jodhpur, the April 10 filing showed.
Applications for grid connectivity totalling about 130 ‚ÄĆGW ‚Ā†have been received in Rajasthan, while transmission systems for only about 73 GW have been planned or are being set up, it added.
"CTUIL is facing challenges and difficulties in identification of corresponding transmission ‚Äčsystem for 60 ‚ÄčGW applications," ‚Ā†the planner said.
The issue was highlighted after electricity regulators told Saurya Urja Company of Rajasthan Ltd, which ‚Äčis developing a 400-megawatt solar park in Bikaner, ‚Äčit ‚Ā†could withdraw its connectivity application and recover bank guarantees if needed.
The ruling by the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) came in response to challenges ‚Ā†in ‚Äčplanning transmission the company faced.
The regulator also ‚Äčasked the transmission planner to advise project applicants about the transmission delays, allowing them ‚Äčto withdraw connectivity applications.

item.title: Iran in touch with India on ships passage through Strait of Hormuz, Iranian envoy says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Iran has "good contact" ‚Äčwith the Indian ‚ÄĆgovernment on passage of the South ‚ÄčAsian nation's ships ‚Äčthrough the Strait of ‚Ā†Hormuz and ‚Äčwants to help New ‚ÄčDelhi, Iranian Ambassador to India Mohammad Fathali ‚Äčsaid on Monday.
The ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ‚ÄĆwhich ‚Ā†began in February, has restricted shipping through the key ‚Äčroute - ‚Äča ‚Ā†conduit for 40% of India's ‚Äčcrude imports - ‚Äčimpacting ‚Ā†trade and squeezing oil supplies.

item.title: Russia calls for joint food reserves with BRICS to counter Middle East crisis risks
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - Russia, the world's largest wheat exporter, should create joint food reserves with ‚Äčfellow BRICS members and former Soviet neighbours to counter the risks to global food security ‚ÄĆstemming from the conflict in the Middle East , a senior Russian security official said on Monday.
About half of the world's food is grown using fertilizer, while one-third of global fertilizer trade used to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow ‚Äčshipping lane along Iran's coast that has been largely closed since the conflict began.
"To ensure food ‚Äčsecurity, it is highly important to expand cooperation with friendly countries, primarily the ‚Ā†member states of the Eurasian Economic Union and BRICS, including through the creation of joint food reserves," ‚ÄčAlexander Maslennikov, deputy secretary of Russia's Security Council, was quoted as saying by domestic news agencies.
Chaired by ‚ÄčPresident Vladimir Putin, the Security Council includes top officials and helps shape Kremlin decisions on major national security issues. Putin is due to meet BRICS member Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto in the Kremlin on Monday with food security likely to feature ‚Äčon the agenda.
Maslennikov said the Middle East crisis posed serious risks to global food security. If the ‚Äčglobal fertilizer shortage persists until early summer, yields of major crops could fall by half, he said, fuelling the sharpest ‚ÄĆrise ‚Ā†in world food inflation in recent years.
He added that the number of hungry people worldwide could rise to a record 673 million.
The World Bank, International Monetary Fund and the U.N. World Food Programme warned last week that sharp increases in oil, natural gas and fertilizer prices triggered by the war in the Middle East ‚Äčwill inevitably cause rising ‚Äčfood prices and food ‚Ā†insecurity.
Russia is a major producer and exporter of fertilizer but lacks the capacity to significantly increase output this year. It is also seeking to raise agricultural ‚Äčexports by half by 2030.
Maslennikov said the current situation, while posing risks to ‚ÄčRussia's own ‚Ā†food security, also created long-term opportunities for the country's agricultural producers.
"Russia is in a strong position to increase food exports to the countries of the Middle East, as well as to Asia, Africa and Latin America," he ‚Ā†said.
Egypt, ‚Äča BRICS member, is the largest importer of Russian wheat, ‚Äčwhile Russia also exports food to China and India, the bloc's two biggest economies. The Eurasian Economic Union, led by Russia, also ‚Äčincludes grain exporter Kazakhstan, as well as Belarus, Armenia and Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Turkey says NATO should reset ties with Trump at next summit, prepare for future
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO allies should use their July summit in Ankara to reset ties with U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump and prepare for a potential reduction of ‚ÄĆU.S. involvement in the alliance.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said that Turkey believed Trump would attend the NATO leaders' summit on July 7-8 due to his "personal ‚Äčrespect" for President Tayyip Erdogan, but added he understood Trump ‚Äčwas otherwise reluctant come to the meeting.
Trump has criticised NATO ‚Ā†for years and last week threatened to pull the United States ‚Äčout of the alliance over European members' refusal to send ships to ‚Äčunblock the Strait of Hormuz near Iran. That compounded friction within the bloc over his earlier plans to acquire Greenland.
Fidan told the state-owned Anadolu news ‚Äčagency that allies had long considered Trump's criticisms to be ‚Äčrhetoric, but were now planning around the possibility of reduced U.S. involvement and ramping ‚ÄĆup ‚Ā†their own defensive capacities.
"NATO countries need to turn this Ankara Summit into an opportunity to put ties with the United States on a systematic basis," he said.
"If there will be a U.S. withdrawal ‚Äčfrom some NATO ‚Äčmechanisms, there needs ‚Ā†to be a plan and programme to phase this out so nobody is left in the open," ‚Äčhe added.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has said ‚Äčhe understood ‚Ā†Trump's frustrations with the alliance, but that the "large majority of European nations" had been helpful to Washington's war effort in Iran.
A senior White ‚Ā†House ‚Äčofficial told Reuters last week that Trump, ‚Äčas part of frustration with NATO, had also considered the option of removing some U.S. ‚Äčtroops from Europe.

item.title: EU member states must coordinate on energy prices amid Iran conflict, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union's member states must coordinate on energy prices amid a ‚Äč22 billion euro ($25.70 billion) increase in fossil fuel ‚ÄĆbills since the start of the Iran war, EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on Monday.
"We're also looking into ... coordination ‚Äčof member states' gas storage filling to avoid ‚Äčthat many member states go to the market ‚Ā†at the same time," von der Leyen told reporters ‚Äčin Brussels.
"And we will coordinate oil stock releases, to achieve ‚Äčthe largest possible effect, and we will ensure that member states' emergency measures will not impact the single market."
The EU Commission is planning ‚Äčto publish proposals for energy price measures on April ‚Äč22, to be discussed by EU leaders at their informal summit ‚ÄĆnext ‚Ā†week.
Separately the EU's executive arm will present an electrification strategy before the summer, von der Leyen said as she stressed the need for structural measures to lower energy prices ‚Äčas well.
"We ‚Äčare paying ‚Ā†a very high price for our global dependency on fossil fuels, and the grim reality ‚Äčfor our continent is fossil fuel energy ‚Äčwill ‚Ā†remain the most expensive option in the years to come," von der Leyen said.
"Our strategy to decarbonize has not only ‚Ā†been ‚Äčconfirmed in the last years, but ‚Äčis growing in importance day by day," she added.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

Under the new reform plan, the commission has set a target to complete one Bangladesh Civil Service (BCS) examination cycle within a year by introducing a structured “Master... Details

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar says he does not back Ukraine's fast-track EU entry
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition ‚Äčleader Peter ‚ÄĆMagyar, whose party won ‚Äčelections on ‚ÄčSunday with a ‚Ā†landslide, ‚Äčsaid on Monday ‚Äčthat his government would not ‚Äčsupport ‚ÄčUkraine's fast-track entry to ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†European Union.
Magyar told a briefing ‚Äčthat ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†issue of Ukraine's ‚Äčmembership would ‚Äčhave ‚Ā†to go to ‚Ā†a ‚Äčreferendum.

item.title: NATO allies refuse to join Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade
item.description:
LONDON/PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States' NATO allies said on Monday they would not get involved in President Donald Trump's plan to blockade ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz , further ratcheting up tensions within the increasingly fragile alliance.
Trump said the U.S. ‚ÄĆmilitary would work with other countries to block all maritime traffic in the waterway, after weekend talks failed to reach an agreement to end the six-week conflict with Iran . The U.S. military later specified that the blockade , due to start at 1400 GMT on Monday, would only apply ‚Äčto ships going to or from Iranian ports.
"The Blockade will begin shortly. Other Countries will be involved with ‚Äčthis Blockade," Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Sunday.
But NATO allies including Britain ‚Ā†and France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, saying instead ‚Äčthat it was vital to open the waterway through which one-fifth of the world's oil usually passes, which Iran has ‚Äčeffectively closed since the conflict began on February 28.
Their refusal to participate is yet another point of friction with Trump, who has threatened to withdraw from the military alliance and is weighing pulling some U.S. troops from Europe after several countries resisted supporting the U.S. campaign against Iran ‚Äčby denying U.S. military planes use of their airspace.
"We're not supporting the blockade," British Prime Minister Keir Starmer ‚Äčtold the BBC.
"My decision has been very clearly that whatever the pressure, and there's been some considerable pressure, we're not getting dragged into ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†war," he said.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told European governments that Trump wants concrete commitments in the near future to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, diplomats told Reuters last week.
NATO could play a role in the strait if its 32 members could agree on the formation of a mission, Rutte said on April 9.
Several European countries have said they're willing to ‚Äčhelp in the strait but ‚Äčonly once there is a ‚Ā†durable end to hostilities and an agreement with Iran that their ships will not be attacked.
France will organise a conference with Britain and other countries to create a multinational mission ‚Äčto restore navigation in the strait, French President Emmanuel Macron said on X on ‚ÄčMonday.
"This strictly defensive ‚Ā†mission, distinct from the belligerents, will be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said.
Britain is working on ways to reduce insurance premiums for ships passing through the strait once the fighting has stopped, according to a senior European official.
The Strait of ‚Ā†Hormuz ‚Äčshould be reopened by diplomacy, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on ‚ÄčMonday, adding that creating an international force to oversee it would be complicated as he called for NATO to reset its ties with Trump at ‚Äča summit in Ankara in July.

item.title: Diplomatic push underway on Hormuz fertiliser proposal, UN says, as shortages bite
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - A diplomatic push is under way on a U.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser ‚Äčshipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the U.N. said on Monday, with ‚ÄĆa source familiar with discussions emphasising growing urgency due to shortages.
The six weeks of fighting sparked by U.S.-Israeli joint strikes on Iran, which spread to Lebanon and the Gulf, have ‚Äčkilled thousands of people across the Middle East, upending global supply chains following ‚Äča near-total halt to shipping via the Strait of Hormuz.
The U.S. ‚Ā†military said it will begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and ‚Äčexiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after failed weekend talks to ‚Äčend the war jeopardized a fragile two-week ceasefire.
There is an urgent need to find a solution, a source familiar with the U.N.-led initiative told Reuters, noting that some farmers ‚Äčin Latin America are already skipping second corn plantings and that African ‚Äčgrowers face a critical window, with severe yield losses looming without fertilisers.
"Extensive meetings" have been ‚ÄĆheld ‚Ā†in New York over the past two weeks on the design and operationalisation of the mechanism with the relevant member states, Juliette Touma, UNOPS director of communications, told Reuters.
The meetings, led by Jorge Moreira da Silva, executive ‚Äčdirector of the United ‚ÄčNations Office for ‚Ā†Project Services, are ongoing, Touma added.
The proposed mechanism , opens new tab seeks to facilitate safe transit of commercial vessels carrying fertilizers and related ‚Äčmaterials through the strait and mitigate potential humanitarian fallout on fertilizer-dependent ‚Äčnations in ‚Ā†Asia and Africa, she said.
A more-than-90% drop in tanker traffic is threatening agricultural production and global food security, the UN Food and Agriculture Organization says.
Businesses seek ‚Ā†a ‚Äčmechanism that would restore freedom of navigation without ‚Äčthe need for naval escorts or tolls, the source familiar with discussions said, adding that the initiative could ‚Äčeventually expand to other goods.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif says 'full effort' still on to resolve US-Iran conflict
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 13 (Reuters) - Full efforts ‚Äčare still ‚ÄĆbeing made to ‚Äčresolve the ‚Äčconflict between the ‚Ā†U.S. ‚Äčand Iran, ‚ÄčPakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčon Monday, a day ‚ÄĆafter ‚Ā†talks between the two ‚Äčsides, ‚Äčheld ‚Ā†in Islamabad, ended ‚Äčwithout ‚Äčagreement.

item.title: Airlines cancel flights amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
April 13 - Global air travel remains severely disrupted, with many people unable to fly as planned to destinations after the Iran war forced the closure of major Middle Eastern hubs, including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi.
Below is the latest on flights, in alphabetical order:
AEGEAN AIRLINES (AGNr.AT) , opens new tab
Greece's largest carrier cancelled flights to Riyadh and Amman until June 27 and to Tel Aviv and Beirut until June 26. It cancelled ‚Äčflights to Erbil and Baghdad until July 2 and to Dubai until June 29.
Latvia's airBaltic says flights to Tel Aviv have been cancelled until May 31. Flights to Dubai ‚ÄĆare cancelled until October 24.
AIR CANADA (AC.TO) , opens new tab
The Canadian carrier has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv and Dubai until September 7.
The Spanish airline has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv until May 3.
AIR FRANCE-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab
Air France has suspended its Tel Aviv, Beirut, Dubai and Riyadh flights until May 3.
KLM has suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until May 17.
CATHAY PACIFIC (0293.HK) , opens new tab
The Hong Kong airline has cancelled flights to Dubai and Riyadh until June 30. To cater for a surge in demand to Europe, it will operate ‚Äčextra passenger flights to London, Paris and Zurich in April.
DELTA (DAL.N) , opens new tab
The U.S. carrier has cancelled its New York-Tel Aviv flights and delayed the restart of its Atlanta-Tel Aviv route until September 5. It ‚Äčsaid the launch of its Boston-Tel Aviv route, planned for late October, has been delayed until further notice.
EL AL ISRAEL AIRLINES (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab
The Israeli carrier said customers who ‚Ā†planned to depart Israel through April 18 have had their flights cancelled, including relevant return flights.
It will increase the number of destinations to about 30 from April 13 and will gradually expand that number through ‚Äčthe rest of the month.
The UAE airline has said it is operating a reduced flight schedule following a partial reopening of regional airspace.
The UAE carrier has said it is operating a commercial flight schedule between ‚ÄčAbu Dhabi and around 80 destinations.
FINNAIR (FIA1S.HE) , opens new tab
The Finnish carrier cancelled its Doha flights until July 2, while continuing to avoid the airspace of Iraq, Iran, Syria and Israel. The airline only restarts its Dubai flights in October.
FLYNAS (4264.SE) , opens new tab
The Saudi budget airline has suspended flights to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Doha, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq and Syria until April 15.
IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab
IAG-owned British Airways is reducing flights to the Middle East when services resume, permanently dropping Jeddah as a destination, while adding capacity to India and Africa.
It ‚Äčplans to reduce services to Dubai, Doha and Tel Aviv to one daily flight from July‚ÄĮ 1, and to cut Riyadh services from two daily flights to one from mid‚ÄĎMay. Changes apply through the summer ‚Äčseason that ends on October 24, with one Dubai service restarting on October 16.
IAG's Spanish low-cost airline Iberia Express has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through May 31.
JAPAN AIRLINES (9201.T) , opens new tab
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until May 10 and ‚ÄĆDoha-Tokyo flights ‚Ā†until May 11. The airline also announced extra flights between Tokyo and London on April 25.
The Polish airline suspended its flights to Tel Aviv until May 31. It also cancelled flights to Riyadh until June 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to May 30. The airline plans to operate its winter route to Dubai in October.
LUFTHANSA GROUP (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab
Lufthansa, Swiss, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, ITA Airways and Edelweiss suspended flights to Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31, and to Abu Dhabi, Amman, Beirut, Dammam, Riyadh, Erbil, Muscat and Tehran until October 24. Lufthansa Cargo is the same, except for the Tel Aviv suspension, which will last through April ‚Äč30.
Low-cost carrier Eurowings plans to suspend flights to Tel ‚ÄčAviv, Beirut and Erbil through April 30 and ‚Ā†to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman through October 24.
The Malaysian carrier suspended flights to Doha until June 14.
NORWEGIAN AIR (NAS.OL) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15.
PEGASUS (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Riyadh, Dubai, Abu Dhabi ‚Äčand Sharjah flights until May 1.
The Moroccan carrier says flights to Doha are cancelled until June 30 and those to Dubai until May ‚Äč31.
QANTAS (QAN.AX) , opens new tab
Australia's flag carrier is adding ‚Ā†flights to Rome and Paris to meet an upswing in demand for European routes. Flights to Paris will increase to five return flights per week from three and the Perth-Singapore service will increase from daily to 10 flights per week. An updated schedule will come into effect progressively for flights from mid-April and run until late July.
The carrier said it is gradually increasing flights from Doha to more than 120 destinations by mid-May.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab
The carrier ‚Ā†extended its ‚ÄčSingapore-Dubai flights suspension until May 31, while adding services on the Singapore-London Gatwick and Singapore-Melbourne routes from late March until October ‚Äč24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab
SunExpress, Turkish Airlines' joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai until April 30.
WIZZ AIR (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline suspended flights to Israel until April 25, and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman from mainland European destinations until mid-September. All ‚Äčflights to Medina have been suspended indefinitely.

item.title: Intergovernmental deal for $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline due this year, official
item.description:
RABAT, April 13 (Reuters) - An intergovernmental agreement (IGA) on a planned $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline will be signed this year, the head of Morocco's hydrocarbons and mining ‚Äčagency (ONHYM) said.
Agreed a decade ago, the project - known as the African ‚ÄĆAtlantic Gas Pipeline - would run 6,900 km on a hybrid offshore-onshore route with a maximum capacity of 30 billion cubic metres (bcm), including 15 bcm to supply Morocco and support exports to ‚ÄčEurope, ONHYM's Amina Benkhadra told Reuters by email.
The pipeline, which has the ‚Äčbacking of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), has completed ‚Ā†its feasibility study and front-end engineering design (FEED) stages.
Following the intergovernmental agreement, a high ‚Äčauthority for the pipeline will be established in Nigeria, bringing together ministerial representatives from ‚Äčeach of the 13 participating countries to provide political and regulatory coordination, Benkhadra said.
A project company will also be created in Morocco as a joint venture between ONHYM and the Nigerian ‚ÄčNational Petroleum Company (NNPC) to lead the execution, financing and construction phase, she said.
The ‚Äčpipeline would spur economic integration across West Africa by expanding electricity generation and facilitating industrial and ‚ÄĆmining ‚Ā†development, while helping Morocco position itself as an energy bridge between Africa and Europe, she said.
Initial segments of the project would connect Morocco to gas fields in Mauritania and Senegal, and link Ghana to Cote d'Ivoire further south, before a final ‚Äčsegment connects Ghana to ‚ÄčNigeria's gas fields, ‚Ā†she said.
First gas from the initial phases is expected in 2031, Benkhadra said.
"The project does not rely on a single ‚Äčglobal final investment decision," she said, adding that each segment ‚Äčis designed ‚Ā†to be developed as "standalone system" to allow for early value build up, she said.
No final funding commitments have been secured yet, she said, adding that the financing structure ‚Ā†will be ‚Äčled by the project company, which will mobilise ‚Äča mix of equity and debt.
"The project is attracting strong interest due to its scale, its phased ‚Äčstructure, and its strategic positioning," Benkhadra said.

item.title: India gets first Iranian oil in 7 years, ship tracking data shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Two very large crude carriers loaded with Iranian oil have reached ‚ÄčIndian ports, ship tracking data from LSEG ‚ÄĆshows, as local refiners utilise a temporary waiver granted by the United States last month to resume purchases from ‚ÄčTehran for the first time in seven ‚Äčyears.
The current waiver is due to expire on ‚Ā†April 19.
The Iran-flagged Felicity has reached Sikka ‚ÄčPort in western India, while the Curacao-flagged Jaya ‚Äčis at the eastern port of Odisha, the data shows.
A VLCC carries 2 million barrels of oil.
India, the world's third-biggest ‚Äčoil importer and consumer, has not received ‚Äča cargo from Iran since May 2019 after coming under ‚ÄĆU.S. ‚Ā†pressure not to buy the country's crude.
Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , the country's top refiner, has bought Iranian oil loaded on the Jaya, a vessel under U.S. ‚Äčsanctions, Reuters ‚Äčreported last ‚Ā†week.
India has also allowed Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab , the operator of the world's ‚Äčbiggest refining complex, to buy Iranian oil ‚Äčloaded ‚Ā†on the Comoros-flagged aframax Kaviz, Curacao-flagged VLCC Lenore and Iran-flagged VLCCs Felicity and Hedy, all of which are ‚Ā†more ‚Äčthan 20 years old and ‚Äčare also under U.S. sanctions.

item.title: UBS raises Brent oil price view as Strait¬†of Hormuz disruptions persist
item.description:
April 13 - The path of least resistance for crude oil prices continues to remain upward as long as flows through the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz stay restricted, UBS said in a note on Monday ‚ÄĆas it raised its Brent crude prices forecasts.
The bank increased its Brent price forecast to $100 a barrel by the end of June 2026, $95 by the end of September, and $90 ‚Äčby the end of December. It also expects Brent to trade at $85 ‚Äča barrel by the end of March 2027.
"There is elevated uncertainty ‚Ā†in respect to these forecasts depending on how the conflict evolves, what ‚Äčenergy infrastructure is disrupted, and when flows through the strait resume," it noted.
A ‚Äčprolonged disruption would raise the risk of a short-term price overshoot, with Brent potentially trading above $150 a barrel, while heightening the likelihood of demand destruction, UBS said.
It added that scarcity-driven ‚Äčhoarding could further amplify price swings.

item.title: Protracted Hormuz crisis could trigger agrifood catastrophe, UN food agency says
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - A prolonged crisis in ‚Äčthe Strait of ‚ÄĆHormuz could trigger a global agrifood ‚Äčcatastrophe by disrupting ‚Äčfertiliser and energy exports, ‚Ā†driving up ‚Äčfood prices and ‚Äčsqueezing crop yields, the U.N. Food and Agriculture ‚ÄčOrganization said ‚Äčon Monday.
FAO Chief Economist Maximo ‚ÄĆTorero ‚Ā†said poorer countries were most exposed because planting ‚Äčcalendars meant ‚Äčdelays ‚Ā†in access to key ‚Äčinputs could quickly ‚Äčtranslate ‚Ā†into lower output, higher inflation ‚Ā†and ‚Äčslower global ‚Äčgrowth.

item.title: Emerging economies in focus at IMF World Bank meetings
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - Global financial policy makers gather in Washington for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group Spring Meetings this week against the backdrop of yet another global economic shock - this time from the Iran war .
The latest economic strife - marked by soaring borrowing costs, energy prices and inflation prospects - will amplify ‚Äčpressure on a number of emerging market nations , from Sri Lanka and Egypt to Pakistan, that had been just emerging from the ‚ÄĆother crises of recent years.
Here is a selection of those and other countries expected to be in focus:
IMF funding is key for Ukraine, which struck a two-year $8.1 billion programme with the Fund in February.
One major external obstacle to keeping IMF funding on track may have been removed: Sunday's election ouster of Hungarian Premier Viktor Orban is expected to pave the way for 90 ‚Äčbillion euros of European Union funding to Kyiv, a precondition for the IMF programme.
However, Ukraine also still needs to pursue an ambitious domestic reform ‚Äčprogramme ranging from raising revenue to tackling corruption and greater exchange rate flexibility.
"The key question domestically is if we see more, ‚Ā†and we do need to see more, efforts by the Ukrainians to pass these pieces of reform, and they seem like they're going there," Roger Mark ‚Äčat asset manager Ninety One said.
The West African nation's economic outlook is still murky due to the discovery of billions in undisclosed debt, which prompted the IMF to halt ‚Äča $1.8 billion loan programme in 2024.
Talks on a new programme are ongoing, but correcting the debt burden would likely require a painful and lengthy fiscal consolidation , which may not be easy for local authorities.
"Without the large fiscal effort, the Fund may ask Senegal for some sort of debt treatment, which has been firmly rejected by the authorities," JPMorgan analysts said in a ‚Äčnote, adding they expected talks between Dakar and the IMF to continue. "Our base case of a new IMF arrangement remains tricky, with a muddle-through scenario as ‚Äčanother option."
Mozambique has been in talks with the IMF since mid-2025 over a new lending programme. The country has said it wants to restructure its debt, though it has yet to ‚ÄĆoutline any ‚Ā†details. In a surprise move, it also repaid dues to the IMF early in March - though that was interpreted as a sign it wanted to secure fresh funds down the line.
"Mozambique's recent payment is a bold attempt to acquire further credit from the IMF," Christian Franken at Oxford Economics said. "Indeed, we expect such a loan agreement to be finalised in Q2 2026."
Gabon's authorities formally requested a Fund programme in March to support their reform agenda. Years of political instability have left Gabon, the ‚Äčsecond-largest CEMAC economy, in an economic crunch with dwindling reserves.
Talks on ‚Äčthe contours of a programme are ‚Ā†expected to continue during the Spring meetings.
In the past two years, Egypt has unlocked billions in IMF funding and foreign direct investment from Gulf states. But heavy exposure to energy imports, GCC remittances and tourism makes Egypt vulnerable to the fallout ‚Äčfrom the Iran war.
Some expect Egypt to ask for an augmentation of its IMF programme which includes an $8‚ÄĮ billion ‚ÄčExtended Fund Facility and ‚Ā†a $1.3 billion Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The Fund is due to review the programme for the next financing tranche in June, but analysts expect delays could push that to later in the year.
Venezuela's $5 billion in special drawing rights - the IMF's own currency - has been frozen since 2021 as its government was not recognised by a majority of IMF ‚Ā†Board Member countries - a ‚Äčstatus that has yet to change under the leadership of President Delcy Rodriguez.
But with the ‚ÄčFund consulting on the matter, this could soon change. A data blackout, which has stymied engagement with multilateral lenders, is also being faded out, with Caracas releasing batches of macroeconomic data in recent weeks. Some analysts ‚Äčsay this could be seen as a precursor to a formal IMF mission visiting Caracas in coming months.

item.title: India to resume visa services for Bangladeshis next week
IEA is ready to further tap global oil reserves if needed, chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The head of the International Energy Agency, ‚ÄĆFatih Birol, said on Monday he hopes another oil stockpile release is not needed but "we stand ready to act" if ‚Äčthe energy shock resulting from the war ‚Äčwith Iran requires it.
The 32-member IEA agreed last ‚Ā†month to release 400 million barrels of oil ‚Äčfrom reserves, the largest coordinated release ever, in a ‚Äčbid to calm oil markets. The U.S., the world's largest oil and gas producer, agreed to release 172 million barrels from ‚Äčits Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"I hope, very much hope, ‚Äčwe don't need to do it but if it is ‚ÄĆneeded ‚Ā†we are ready to act," Birol said.
Birol reiterated at an Atlantic Council event that the war has resulted in the worst global energy disruption ever and ‚Äčsaid that more ‚Äčthan 80 ‚Ā†oil and gas facilities including production, terminals and refineries across the Middle East have been ‚Äčdamaged by war with Iran. Benchmark oil ‚Äčprices ‚Ā†are trading near $100 a barrel.
Due to the vast extent of the production shut-ins and closure of the Strait ‚Ā†of ‚ÄčHormuz, the oil releases are "not ‚Äča solution," Birol said, "it's just reducing the pain."

item.title: Russia‚Äôs Rosneft diverts oil to Tuapse refinery from drone-hit Novorossiysk port, sources say
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Rosneft is diverting oil supplies to the Tuapse refinery from the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk after the terminal, which handles roughly 14% of Russia's crude exports, was heavily ‚Äčdamaged in a drone attack, three sources said on Monday.
A Ukrainian drone attack ‚ÄĆsparked a large fire at the Sheskharis oil terminal in the Novorossiysk port last week, damaging two of its main berths and forcing oil exports from the port to halt, according to the sources, who ‚Äčwere familiar with the matter but were not authorised to speak to the ‚Äčmedia.
The development underscores Russia's challenges in exporting oil as the rising number of ‚Ā†Ukrainian drone attacks deals damage to its energy infrastructure, putting additional pressure on the ‚ÄčRussian pipeline system and increasing the risk of disruptions to crude oil output.
Russian oil producer Rosneft, ‚Äčone of the country's largest exporters shipping crude via Novorossiysk, diverted a significant volume of oil to its Tuapse refinery, two of the sources said.
Runs at the refinery, which is located close to the Novorossiysk ‚Äčport, are expected to increase to nearly 800,000 metric tons this month, they added, from ‚Äčusual 500,000-600,000 tons per month.
Tuapse processed about 500,000 tons in March, the sources said. The refinery is focused ‚ÄĆon fuel ‚Ā†exports and ramping up its capacity makes sense amid higher margins, they added.
Rosneft did not respond to a request for comment.
Other exporters transporting oil via the port have begun diverting crude volumes to alternative destinations, including other export routes and domestic refineries, the sources added, saying ‚Äčthey do not expect ‚Äča quick resolution.
They ‚Ā†are likely to redirect oil to Baltic ports that have resumed loadings after the attacks or also to the domestic market, the sources ‚Äčsaid.
Novorossiysk, which can export about 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, resumed ‚Äčlimited oil ‚Ā†loadings last week, but traders said the flows were minimal and the port was not operating anywhere near full capacity.
The loadings resumed for berth number two, which accommodates smaller tankers, while the ‚Ā†two main ‚Äčoil export berths remained shut.
Moscow ramped up oil loadings in ‚Äčearly April and exported more crude on average than in March, Reuters reported last week, helping to offset part ‚Äčof the losses caused by disruptions in the Black Sea.

item.title: EU launches operations of critical minerals procurement platform
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union on Monday launched the critical minerals section of its energy and materials procurement platform, which aims ‚Äčto give more power to regional buyers and cut dependence on ‚ÄĆdominant producer China by aggregating their purchases.
The platform is part of the bloc's RESourceEU strategy , announced in December to develop its supply chains for rare earths and other ‚Äčstrategic minerals needed for the energy transition and defence applications. China ‚Äčcontrols up to 90% of output in that sector.
The EU's Energy and Raw Materials Platform , opens new tab opened to submissions from buyers on ‚ÄčMonday in the first phase of a multi-month process, an EU spokesperson ‚Äčtold Reuters.
The platform connects buyers with suppliers, but leaves the two sides to finalise trades.
The first round of connections between buyers and sellers will start on April 13, ‚Äčthe spokesperson said, and will focus on "immediately and soon-to-be-available rare earths, battery ‚Äčand defence raw materials". The results will be announced in September.
Last year, the EU chose ‚ÄĆPriceWaterhouseCoopers ‚Ā†and a Slovak software company to develop its 9 million euro platform, which has separate mechanisms for strategic raw materials, hydrogen, and energy products such as natural gas and biomethane.
The platform kicked off with hydrogen, which started submissions ‚Äčin November and announced ‚Äčresults , opens new tab on April ‚Ā†1, with 273 match-ups.
The REsourceEU plan aims to accelerate the bloc's Critical Raw Materials Act, passed in 2023. ‚ÄčThe EU wants to speed up the development of ‚Äčits own ‚Ā†supply chains so as not to rely on any single country for more than 65% of its demand.
Under the CRMA, the bloc has set ambitious ‚Ā†2030 targets ‚Äčfor domestic production of critical minerals required ‚Äčfor its green transition - 10% of annual needs mined, 25% recycled and 40% processed domestically ‚Äčby the end of the decade.

item.title: South Africa agrees new climate loan, critical minerals cooperation on Germany visit
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Germany has agreed to give South Africa ‚ÄĆa new 200 million euro ($234 million) concessional climate loan, and the two countries will deepen cooperation in ‚Äčother areas like critical minerals, South Africa's ‚Äčforeign minister said on Monday.
The loan will ‚Ā†support investment in South Africa's power grid ‚Äčand renewable energy capacity, Minister Ronald Lamola said ‚Äčafter talks with German counterpart Johann Wadephul in Berlin.
He said German and European Union funding for green hydrogen and ‚Äčbattery value chain cooperation had also been ‚Äčextended by more than 270 million euros.
Lamola thanked Wadephul ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†German support despite South Africa's strained relationship with the United States during President Donald Trump's second term in office.
Trump has excluded South Africa from meetings ‚Äčof the ‚ÄčGroup of ‚Ā†20 nations this year and criticised its foreign policy and domestic ‚Äčrace laws. He boycotted a G20 summit in ‚ÄčJohannesburg ‚Ā†in November.
Lamola said: "We feel we are part of it (the G20) because of the support that we ‚Ā†have ‚Äčreceived from Germany and from ‚Äčother G20 members."
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Hezbollah chief urges Lebanese government to cancel Washington talks with Israel
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 13 (Reuters) - Hezbollah chief Naim ‚ÄčQassem on Monday ‚ÄĆcalled on the Lebanese government to ‚Äčcancel a ‚ÄčTuesday meeting between ‚Ā†the Lebanese and ‚ÄčIsraeli ambassadors ‚Äčin Washington, describing the talks as pointless.
In ‚Äča televised ‚Äčspeech, Qassem said the ‚ÄĆarmed ‚Ā†group will continue to confront Israeli attacks on ‚ÄčLebanon.
Lebanon's ‚Äčforeign ‚Ā†minister said Beirut would ‚Äčuse Tuesday's face-to-face ‚Äčnegotiations ‚Ā†to press for a ceasefire ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe war.
